event.title: ShinyHunters → Anodot → Snowflake supply-chain credential breach
event.status: Active extortion → public dumps for non-paying victims
event.summary: In April 2026 the extortion group ShinyHunters compromised Anodot (an AI business-analytics / anomaly-detection SaaS) and harvested the OAuth/API tokens Anodot uses to query its customers' cloud data warehouses. Using those legitimate, trusted credentials, the actors logged directly into victim Snowflake (and some BigQuery) instances — bypassing MFA and perimeter controls — and exfiltrated data that looked like routine Anodot queries, delaying detection. Confirmed Anodot-vector victims include Rockstar Games, Vimeo, Zara/Inditex, and 7-Eleven, among others. This is a third-party / supply-chain breach: Snowflake's own architecture was not breached.

review.expertise: Snowflake expertise from working as an enterprise data scientist with Snowflake
review.note: Reviewed the attack chain, victim list, and record-count figures against the cited sources; the trusted-integration framing matches real Snowflake integration patterns. The false-alarm exclusions are correctly scoped by vector, not just actor — which is the distinction that actually matters here.

ai.basis: A trusted-integration token compromise that bypassed MFA and perimeter controls at multiple major enterprises and exposed real customer PII (Vimeo, Zara/Inditex) — a systemic SaaS-supply-chain risk. Tempered below 'Critical' because the headline figure (Rockstar's 78.6M) was low-materiality telemetry, not credentials, payment data, or source code.

human.basis: From a Snowflake practitioner's seat, the danger isn't the record counts — it's that a trusted integration's service account silently bypassed MFA and network controls, and the exfiltration looked like ordinary analytics queries. Every enterprise running a SaaS analytics vendor against its warehouse has this exposure right now, and most haven't scoped down or rotated those integration tokens. High — trending Critical for any org that hasn't audited its third-party warehouse grants.

severity.note: Severity is context-conditioned (VISION §5): raw record counts are not materiality. AI assigns a baseline; each named expert may re-assess from their domain point of view.

diagrams.title: The trusted-integration supply-chain breach
diagrams.caption: The attack chain: compromise the vendor → steal its OAuth tokens → walk through the front door of every warehouse it was trusted to query → exfiltrate disguised as normal queries → extort.

diagrams.title: Big count, low materiality
diagrams.caption: The reconciliation: 78.6M records (the hacker's headline) is repetitive machine telemetry — no source code, no credentials, no payment cards. Volume ≠ materiality.

diagrams.title: One compromise, many victims (the blast radius)
diagrams.caption: Compromise one trusted vendor and you reach every warehouse it was trusted to query. Any vendor holding tokens into your Snowflake/BigQuery is part of your attack surface.

timeline.body: ShinyHunters claims a Rockstar Games breach "via Snowflake and Anodot"; sets a "pay or leak" deadline of Apr 14.
timeline.src: SC Media · The Register · The Record · TechCrunch · IGN

timeline.body: Rockstar confirms a third-party breach; calls it "limited, non-material company information." Hacker claim: 78.6M records.
ShinyHunters adds Zara/Inditex and 7-Eleven to the Anodot-victim list; the extortion list grows.
Vimeo confirms loss; final count 119,000 records (emails / titles / metadata).

entities.name: ShinyHunters
entities.role: Extortion group — Tor leak portal, 3–4 day deadlines

entities.name: Anodot
entities.role: AI analytics / anomaly-detection SaaS — the actual point of compromise: the trusted bridge

entities.name: Snowflake
entities.role: Victim data warehouse — queried via stolen integration tokens
entities.status: Locked accounts referencing the compromised integration

entities.name: OAuth / API token theft
entities.role: Trusted-credential database ingress — bypasses MFA + perimeter

entities.name: Telemetry / metrics
entities.role: Rockstar — automated in-game revenue / anti-cheat / support analytics

entities.name: Customer email + metadata
entities.role: Vimeo — emails, video titles, technical metadata

entities.name: Customer PII + orders
entities.role: Zara — customer PII and order data

relationships.subject: ShinyHunters
relationships.rationale: The breach originated at the SaaS integrator, not at any victim or platform.

relationships.subject: ShinyHunters
relationships.rationale: Stolen Anodot OAuth/API tokens were used to log directly into victim warehouses.

relationships.subject: Anodot
relationships.rationale: The long-lived integration tokens are what made the trusted-credential ingress possible.

relationships.subject: breach
relationships.rationale: Data ranged from low-materiality telemetry (Rockstar) to customer PII (Zara, Vimeo).

relationships.subject: ShinyHunters
relationships.rationale: Reported lateral movement attempt, said to be mostly blocked.

reliability.outlets: The Record (Recorded Future), TechCrunch, The Register, SC Media, Help Net Security, Security Affairs, Bitdefender
reliability.note: The attack-chain and response facts rest on this tier.
Incident-response detail and corroboration, read through a vendor lens.

reliability.outlets: IGN, Yahoo, TechSpot, Gadget Review, tech-insider.org, Mathrubhumi
reliability.note: Notability / reach signal — NOT load-bearing for any technical claim.

saturation: 108 distinct-title articles across the lanes. The breadth itself — specialist + mainstream + gaming press all covering it — is the escalation cue that this is a real event worth a report.

conflicts.head: Rockstar: "78.6M records" (hacker)
conflicts.recon: Both true at different layers. The 78.6M are automated telemetry rows — in-game revenue, anti-cheat, and support analytics monitored by Anodot — repetitive machine data with no source code, credentials, or payment cards. Massive count, low materiality.

conflicts.head: Vimeo: "millions at risk" (early)
conflicts.recon: Anodot only processed a subset of accounts for analysis. The final 119k = emails + video titles + technical metadata, not video files or credentials.

related.title: 2024 Snowflake customer-credential campaign (UNC5537)
related.body: Same platform, different vector (stolen customer creds vs. stolen SaaS-integrator tokens). The 2026 event is the same threat surface, evolved — credential abuse against Snowflake, now one hop upstream at the integrator.

related.title: ShinyHunters' broader 2025–26 extortion spree
related.body: Salesforce-linked campaigns and a growing leak-list. This breach is one node in an ongoing actor campaign, not a standalone.

related.title: Pattern: SaaS-integrator-as-vector
related.body: The trusted-third-party-with-warehouse-tokens class (Anodot here) is the generalizable lesson — any vendor holding OAuth tokens into your Snowflake/BigQuery is in-scope.

false_alarms_intro: Knowing what is not part of an event is as valuable as knowing what is. ShinyHunters ran several parallel campaigns in this window, so early coverage swept many companies into "the Anodot breach" — but each below was actually hit by a different vector. Each surfaced as a candidate through the relating step, was investigated, and ruled out of the Anodot event. They are recorded as first-class dispositions, not silently dropped: a downgrade is a finding, and conflating adjacent incidents is how a watch service loses trust.

false_alarms.name: Carnival Corporation
false_alarms.surfaced: Listed on ShinyHunters' "pay or leak" portal in mid-April 2026, alongside the genuine Anodot victims, inside the same extortion wave.
false_alarms.reason: Real breach — but the vector was a phishing / social-engineering compromise of an employee account (Holland America / Mariner Society loyalty data), NOT the Anodot integration. Same actor, different mechanism: a separate incident, not part of this event.

false_alarms.name: Vercel
false_alarms.surfaced: A threat actor claiming to be ShinyHunters took credit for a Vercel breach and offered the data during the same wave.
false_alarms.reason: Real breach — but through a different SaaS integrator (Context.ai, via Lumma Stealer on a Context.ai employee), NOT Anodot. And ShinyHunters leadership denied involvement, so even the actor attribution is disputed (likely an impersonator). Out on both vector and actor.

false_alarms.name: Instructure / Canvas LMS
false_alarms.surfaced: A ~275M-record (3.65 TB) breach claimed by ShinyHunters in the same window — the largest figure in the wave, so widely linked to the Anodot story.
false_alarms.reason: The vector was a Canvas "Free-for-Teacher" platform logic exploit against the LMS itself — NOT stolen Anodot tokens. A separate platform breach; Instructure rotated keys and shut the FFT program.

false_alarms.name: McGraw Hill
false_alarms.surfaced: ~13.5M records leaked by ShinyHunters in mid-April 2026, named alongside the Anodot victims as another edtech/cloud hit.
false_alarms.reason: The vector was a Salesforce Experience Cloud (Aura endpoint) misconfiguration exposing guest-readable objects — NOT Anodot. Different platform, different mechanism.

false_alarms.name: Cisco
false_alarms.surfaced: Named in the same coverage with a ShinyHunters threat to leak millions of Salesforce records, AWS buckets, and source code.
false_alarms.reason: Hit via multiple non-Anodot vectors — UNC6040 voice phishing (vishing), Salesforce Aura harvesting, and a Trivy developer-tool supply-chain compromise. Same actor cluster, none of them Anodot.

false_alarms.name: TELUS / TELUS Digital
false_alarms.surfaced: A very large (reportedly ~1 PB) ShinyHunters breach with BigQuery data exfiltration, mentioned in the same Snowflake / warehouse coverage.
false_alarms.reason: The access path was a 2025 Drift/Salesloft OAuth-token compromise → leaked GCP credentials → BigQuery (mined with TruffleHog) — NOT Anodot. A multi-hop credential chain, separate event.

mitigations.t: Revoke + rotate all third-party integration OAuth tokens; treat long-lived integration tokens as crown jewels.
Monitor integration query patterns — exfiltration hid inside "normal Anodot queries"; baseline and alert on volume/shape anomalies from integration principals.
Scope-down integration grants — least-privilege per-warehouse, short-lived tokens.
Platform-side containment — Snowflake locked accounts referencing the compromised integration; Anodot force-shut all connectors globally.

provenance: 108 Google News RSS items (Snowflake security lanes, retrieved 2026-06-12) + grounded-search supplement. Reliability assessment is a first-pass tiering, not a finished bias model. Record counts and the attack-chain are corroborated across ≥3 specialist sources; the Salesforce-pivot claim is single-tier and marked medium.
